dialogue.line: No, no, no—that's what I'm telling you. I've inhabited this soldier so completely, I can't distinguish between reality and his trauma anymore. I AM him.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. Wait a minute. Let me get a pack of gum. — Can I get a pack of gum, please? — I beg your pardon, Your Majesty, but we don't accept bills with lipstick on the president. — What?
jokes.setup: George, in full Henry VIII costume, tries to buy gum and pays with a bill.
jokes.punchline: The clerk refuses the bill because it has 'lipstick on the president' — which is the bill with 'big lips' doodled on it that George claimed as evidence against Ruthie Cohen. The bill was in his possession all along.
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural masterwork. George has been pursuing Ruthie Cohen, losing his car, nearly being beaten up, all over a $20 he accused the cashier of stealing — and the bill was in his wallet the whole time. 'Lipstick on the president' is the perfect formulation: the clerk's tactful description of George's own doodle. The reversal is perfectly earned, and the setup was laid 11 minutes earlier. This is Seinfeld plotting at its best — simultaneously the episode's ironic climax and a devastating character indictment. Comparable to the 9+ anchor examples.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: He's not here.

dialogue.speaker: Jeffrey Haarwood
dialogue.line: Where is he?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: He ran out of here like his hair was on fire.

jokes.text: 'So I had it all along. How do you like that? I guess I owe that cashier an apology.'
jokes.setup: George has just realized he had the $20 bill all along
jokes.punchline: 'I guess I owe that cashier an apology' — delivered with NO apparent remorse, just mild intellectual acknowledgment; George's 'apology' is as minimal as possible for someone who accused a cashier of horse-funded theft
jokes.explanation: 'I guess I owe that cashier an apology' with the flat, emotionless delivery is a perfect George character moment. There's no guilt, no shame, just an academic acknowledgment of a social obligation. The 'how do you like that' preceding it suggests George finds the whole thing mildly amusing rather than mortifying. Pure George Costanza.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh, I got it, I got it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna get your button.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, just be careful.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh, don't worry about it.

jokes.text: Deena sees George in the Henry VIII costume. 'Oh, my God.' 'No, no, Deena, it's not what you think. This isn't mine. I got it from the Institute.' 'The Institute!'
jokes.setup: Deena has been growing increasingly convinced George is having a breakdown; she sees him dressed as Henry VIII
jokes.punchline: 'I got it from the Institute.' 'The Institute!' — Deena's horrified reaction to 'the Institute' (which she understands as a mental institution, not the I.P.M.P.C.W.) is the perfect misunderstanding; George's costume is now evidence of a complete break from reality
jokes.explanation: The episode's comedic apex and a masterpiece of structural comedy. 'I got it from the Institute' is the truth — the I.P.M.P.C.W. — but Deena hears 'mental institution.' All of George's genuinely true explanations (woman on horse, man with flowers, fooling Lloyd Braun, Institute) have sounded progressively more insane throughout the episode, and the Institute is the final nail. George dressed as Henry VIII, explaining he got the costume from 'the Institute,' confirms everything Deena feared. The episode's entire thematic argument — that the truth is indistinguishable from madness in this world — pays off here. Justifies near-top scores for structure, character integration, and the elegant double-meaning of 'the Institute.' Among the best Seinfeld structural jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, Elaine needs some romantic companionship.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, she really does.

jokes.text: Kramer notices Elaine's button on Mr. Haarwood's lapel. He wants it. 'Shall I undo it?' 'Yes, of course.' 'I'm a little ticklish.' 'Tickle, tickle.'
jokes.setup: Elaine's long-lost antique ivory button has been found on Mr. Haarwood's lapel
jokes.punchline: Mr. Haarwood admits he's ticklish as Elaine undoes the button; Elaine says 'Tickle, tickle' — an absurdly playful, childlike moment between two adults in a formal setting
jokes.explanation: Warm, slightly absurd button-recovery scene. 'Tickle, tickle' from Elaine is endearingly odd. The plot resolution is satisfying and the physical comedy of undoing an elderly man's button with tickling is a nice light closer. Rewatch bonus for tracking the button's journey.

jokes.text: Kramer watching Elaine's button exchange with Haarwood: 'We really gotta get that Elaine a boyfriend.' Kramer: 'Oh, tell me about it.'
jokes.setup: Jerry and Kramer observe Elaine flirtatiously interacting with the elderly Mr. Haarwood over a button
jokes.punchline: Jerry's 'We really gotta get that Elaine a boyfriend' — treating her button-retrieval interaction with an old man as evidence of romantic desperation; Kramer's sincere agreement compounds it
jokes.explanation: Strong closing tag. Jerry and Kramer misread Elaine's practical button retrieval as flirtation with an elderly man, mirroring Lloyd's misread of the button blouse earlier. The line closes the episode on a breezy character note. 'We really gotta get that Elaine a boyfriend' has good rhythm and quotability.

jokes.text: Tickle, tickle.
jokes.setup: Elaine says she's ticklish as Kramer undoes her blouse button.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's 'Tickle, tickle' — presumably accompanied by a physical gesture — is a perfectly absurd non-sequitur from a man who was just given permission to undo a woman's blouse button. It's baby talk in an adult moment.
jokes.explanation: Two words of pure Kramer. 'Tickle, tickle' as a response to an adult woman saying she's ticklish during a blouse-unbuttoning is a perfect character beat — childlike, innocent, slightly inappropriate, completely Kramer. The economy is exceptional.

tmdb_overview: Kramer is overprotective of a friend who had a breakdown after dating Elaine; a former neighbor is worried about George's mental stability.
insight: Gum obsession drives 74 jokes in 22 minutes, but escalation never quite peaks.
